---
title: "08 · Scientific Notation"
subtitle: "Writing huge and tiny numbers compactly"
lesson-id: "t2.scientific-notation"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t2.exponents-powers", "t1.decimals"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["systems", "research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 20
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [07 · Exponents](../07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)
   and [T1 L08 · Decimals](../../tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $10^3 = 1000$ and $10^{-2} = 0.01$?
3. Have you seen something like "1.5 × 10⁸" on a science textbook?
:::

## Touch

Switch a number between *normal* and *scientific* form.

```{ojs}
viewof rawValue = Inputs.range([-15, 15], {value: 6, step: 1, label: "Power of 10:"})
viewof mantissa = Inputs.range([1, 9.9], {value: 3.5, step: 0.1, label: "Mantissa (1 ≤ m < 10):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const value = mantissa * Math.pow(10, rawValue);
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.6rem;">
      <span style="color:#c2185b;font-weight:bold;">${mantissa.toFixed(1)}</span>
      &nbsp;×&nbsp;
      <span style="color:#2c5f7c;font-weight:bold;">10<sup>${rawValue}</sup></span>
      &nbsp;=&nbsp;
      <span style="color:#1f2933;">${value.toExponential(4)}</span>
    </div>
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.2rem; margin-top:0.5rem; color:#666;">
      = ${value < 1e-4 || value >= 1e15 ? value.toExponential(2) : value.toLocaleString(undefined, {maximumFractionDigits: 8})}
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

The form $a \times 10^n$ with $1 \le a < 10$ is **scientific notation**.
$a$ is the **mantissa**, $n$ is the **exponent**. Try the speed of
light: $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

## Wonder

1. Set mantissa to 1 and power to 100. The number is enormous. Could
   you even write it out longhand?
2. Set mantissa to 6.022 and power to 23. That's **Avogadro's number**
   — the count of atoms in 12 g of carbon-12. Why would you want
   notation for such a number?
3. Set the power to $-9$. What is "1 nanometre" as $10^{-9}$ metre?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Write in normal form:

(a) $4 \times 10^3$
(b) $5.7 \times 10^5$
(c) $2 \times 10^{-3}$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) $4000$. (b) $570{,}000$. (c) $0.002$.

Each step in the exponent moves the decimal point one place. Positive
exponent → move right (bigger number). Negative exponent → move left
(smaller number).
</details>

### Problem 2

Convert to scientific notation:

(a) $73{,}000{,}000$
(b) $0.000045$
(c) $1{,}234$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The mantissa must be in $[1, 10)$. Move the decimal point to make this
true; count how many places.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) $7.3 \times 10^7$. (b) $4.5 \times 10^{-5}$. (c) $1.234 \times 10^3$.

Count the places carefully — the most common error is being off by one.
</details>

### Problem 3

Multiply: $(2 \times 10^4) \times (3 \times 10^5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply mantissas; add exponents.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(2 \times 3) \times 10^{4+5} = 6 \times 10^9$.

Scientific notation makes multiplication of huge numbers trivial.
$200{,}000 \times 30{,}000 = 6{,}000{,}000{,}000$ — you'd have to count
zeros without it.
</details>

### Problem 4

Multiply: $(5 \times 10^4) \times (4 \times 10^3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$5 \times 4 = 20$, but the mantissa must be < 10. So renormalise.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(5 \times 4) \times 10^{4+3} = 20 \times 10^7$. Renormalise: $20 = 2
\times 10^1$, so result $= 2 \times 10^8$.

Always finish by ensuring the mantissa is in $[1, 10)$. The "carry"
from mantissa to exponent is just place-value bookkeeping.
</details>

### Problem 5

The mass of an electron is about $9.1 \times 10^{-31}$ kg. The mass of
the Sun is about $2 \times 10^{30}$ kg. By **what factor** is the Sun
heavier than the electron?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Divide. Mantissas divide; exponents subtract.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{2 \times 10^{30}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}} = \dfrac{2}{9.1} \times
10^{30 - (-31)} = 0.22 \times 10^{61} \approx 2.2 \times 10^{60}$.

The Sun is about **$2.2 \times 10^{60}$ times** as heavy as an
electron. Numbers like this — spanning *60 orders of magnitude* — are
literally unwritable without scientific notation.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Archimedes** (3rd century BCE), in his short work *The Sand Reckoner*
(*Psammites*), set out to count the number of grains of sand needed to
fill the universe. The Greek number system topped out at the
"myriad-myriad" ($10^8$), so Archimedes invented his own system of
**orders of magnitude** — essentially a powers-of-$10^8$ scheme — and
estimated the answer at around $10^{63}$ grains. Two and a half
thousand years before "scientific notation" got its name, he was
already doing it.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Scientific notation splits a number into its *shape* (the mantissa,
between 1 and 10) and its *size* (the power of 10). Once split, you
can compare and compute across 60 orders of magnitude as easily as you
compare 3 and 7.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Scientific notation** writes a number as
$$a \times 10^n$$
with $1 \le a < 10$ and $n$ an integer.

- $a$ is the **mantissa** (or *significand*).
- $n$ is the **exponent** (or *order of magnitude*).

Operations:

- **Multiply:** mantissas multiply, exponents add. Renormalise if
  mantissa ≥ 10.
- **Divide:** mantissas divide, exponents subtract.
- **Add/subtract:** convert to the *same* exponent first, then add the
  mantissas.

The notation lets you compare orders of magnitude *just by looking at
the exponent*.

:::

> *Mantissa between one and ten; exponent says how big or small;*
> *Multiply — add exponents; divide — subtract;*
> *Add only after exponents agree.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $(3 \times 10^7) + (4 \times 10^6)$.

A competent solver puts both terms in the *same* exponent first:

1. $4 \times 10^6 = 0.4 \times 10^7$.
2. Now add the mantissas: $3 \times 10^7 + 0.4 \times 10^7 = 3.4 \times
   10^7$.

**Answer: $3.4 \times 10^7$.** Addition (and subtraction) requires
matched exponents — analogous to needing a common denominator for
fractions (Tier 1 Lesson 7).

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Write in normal form: $1.2 \times 10^4$, $7 \times 10^{-2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$12{,}000$, $0.07$.
</details>

**W2.** Write in scientific notation: $345{,}000$, $0.0006$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3.45 \times 10^5$, $6 \times 10^{-4}$.
</details>

**W3.** Compute $(3 \times 10^4) \times (2 \times 10^3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6 \times 10^7$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute $(8 \times 10^5) \div (2 \times 10^2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{8}{2} \times 10^{5-2} = 4 \times 10^3$.
</details>

**P2.** Compute $(7 \times 10^4) + (3 \times 10^5)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Match exponents.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7 \times 10^4 = 0.7 \times 10^5$. Add: $0.7 + 3 = 3.7$. Answer:
$3.7 \times 10^5 = 370{,}000$.
</details>

**P3.** The distance from Earth to the Sun is about $1.5 \times 10^{11}$
m. Light travels $3 \times 10^8$ m/s. How long does light from the Sun
take to reach Earth?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Time = distance ÷ speed.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.5 \times 10^{3} = 500$
seconds, or about **8.3 minutes**.

Sunlight you see right now left the Sun about 8 minutes ago. Scientific
notation makes that calculation a one-liner.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A neural network has **1 trillion parameters** ($10^{12}$). Each
parameter is stored as a 32-bit floating-point number (4 bytes). What
is the total storage required, in **gigabytes** ($10^9$ bytes)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total bytes $= 10^{12} \times 4$. Divide by $10^9$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Bytes $= 4 \times 10^{12}$. Gigabytes $= 4 \times 10^{12} / 10^9 = 4
\times 10^3 = 4000$ GB = **4 terabytes**.

Modern AI models routinely require terabytes of storage. The largest
research models in 2025 have over $10^{13}$ parameters — *tens of
terabytes* just for the weights.

This is why GPU memory and high-bandwidth networking matter so much:
moving weights around is the bottleneck of large-scale training.
</details>

**S2.** Floating-point on a computer is *itself* essentially scientific
notation — a mantissa and an exponent stored separately. A 32-bit float
uses **23 bits for mantissa** and **8 bits for exponent** (plus 1 for
sign). What is the largest representable number, *roughly*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
With 8 exponent bits, the exponent can range up to about ±127. The
maximum value is mantissa × $2^{127}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\approx 2^{127} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{38}$. The standard IEEE 754
single-precision range is from about $\pm 10^{-38}$ to $\pm 10^{38}$.

This is why scientific computing uses **double-precision** (64-bit) for
work that needs greater range or accuracy — exponent fields of 11 bits,
allowing values up to $\sim 10^{308}$.

Floating-point representation is *applied scientific notation* in
silicon. The same mathematics you just learned governs how every
calculator, phone, and supercomputer stores fractional numbers.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$1.5 \times 10^3$ is bigger than $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ because the
   first has a positive exponent."** True here, but **mantissas
   matter**. $9 \times 10^3$ vs $1 \times 10^4$: the exponent of the
   second is bigger, so it wins (10000 > 9000).
2. **"You can add scientific-notation numbers by adding both parts."**
   No — you must align exponents first, then add only mantissas.
3. **"Scientific notation is just a fancy way to show big numbers."**
   It also works for *tiny* numbers (negative exponents) and for any
   number whose order of magnitude is unintuitive.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **[Scientific notation](/glossary.qmd#scientific-notation) writes
   any number as $a \times 10^n$ with $1 \le a < 10$ — a shape and a
   size, kept separate.**
2. **Multiply mantissas and add exponents; divide mantissas and
   subtract exponents; align exponents *before* adding or subtracting.**
3. **The [exponent](/glossary.qmd#order-of-magnitude) tells you the
   *order of magnitude* — that one number lets you compare things 60
   zeros apart at a glance.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you compute $(2 \times 10^5) \times (5 \times 10^{-3})$ in
   your head?
2. Without scrolling back, can you convert $0.0042$ to scientific
   notation?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answers: $10^3 = 1000$; $4.2 \times 10^{-3}$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[09 · Square Roots and Irrationals](../09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** — billions and trillions of parameters in ML
  systems is now standard.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[07 · Exponents and Powers](../07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)**
- **[T1 L08 — Decimals](../../tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 3 — Logarithms** — the *inverse* of exponentials; pH, decibels,
  Richter scale.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems.** Floating-point numbers (Stretch S2) are scientific
notation in binary. Numerical precision, range, overflow, and
underflow are all consequences.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Almost every constant in physics is
quoted in scientific notation: Planck's constant ($6.6 \times 10^{-34}$),
gravitational constant ($6.7 \times 10^{-11}$), speed of light
($3 \times 10^8$), etc.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Model parameter counts ($10^{12}$),
training compute (FLOPs $\sim 10^{24}$), dataset sizes ($10^{14}$
tokens) — all use scientific notation, often via the abbreviations
"trillion," "exa-," etc.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Astronomy (light-years, parsecs), biology
(cell counts, gene lengths), economics (national budgets,
populations), pharmacology (microgram doses) — any field with
extreme scales.
:::
